guideline_references: Therapeutic Guidelines (Australia) — Constipation and faecal impaction
eviQ / RACGP guidance on opioid-induced constipation
ASCRS / general surgical guidance on large-bowel obstruction red flags
intentional_test_features: Diagnostic overshadowing / anchoring trap: a chronic, benign-sounding label ('chronic constipation') that has acutely decompensated into a possible subacute large-bowel obstruction — the case tests whether the system reads the acute red flags (bilious vomiting, distension, obstipation) rather than dismissing it as 'just constipation'.

Overflow-diarrhoea trap: overflow faecal soiling is mistaken by the patient for diarrhoea — the scored error of commission is prescribing an anti-diarrhoeal (e.g. loperamide), which would worsen the impaction.

Telehealth reprojection: the source is an in-person clinician who palpated a faecal mass and performed a digital rectal examination and administered an enema; the AI Doctor cannot examine or administer, so the serious mimics (mechanical obstruction, ischaemia, perforation) cannot be excluded remotely and the correct action is to arrange urgent in-person/ED assessment.
Deprescribing test: codeine is the driver (opioid-induced dysmotility) and must be ceased — the case rewards recognising the medication cause, not just treating the symptom.
Source category: DCD (Disabilities & Congenital Disorders) folder; the source note itself states no explicit congenital/developmental condition, so none is asserted — flagged.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-17)
